summary: Armin argues that companies already tolerate “accidental” spend on infrastructure add-ons, yet funding open source remains a deliberate, scrutinized purchase. He suggests reframing open source support as a predictable, baseline obligation, akin to a tax or tip, to normalize giving. However, he doubts a universal “open source tax” collected by platforms would solve distribution because project value is context-specific and many projects lack governance to receive funds. As an alternative, he proposes the Open Source Pledge: donate an amount based on developer headcount to create an objective, visible baseline for internal conversations. The conclusion is that intentional funding is still essential, but a standardized baseline can make it easier to start and scale support.
key_points: “Accidental spending” on cloud services scales quietly, while open source funding is treated as an intentional, high-friction purchase.
A platform-collected tax could normalize giving but risks misallocating funds and faces governance readiness issues.
Dependency value is highly specific; download counts or dependency graphs can misrepresent criticality.
A developer-count-based pledge offers a quantifiable baseline that aligns funding with business scale.
The pledge model is framed like a tip: guidance without removing choice, enabling internal advocacy.
money_quotes: I call those purchases “accidental” because they are proportional to the deployed infrastructure and largely acting like a tax on top of everything.
Open Source funding could be seen through both of these lenses.
Companies are unlikely to fund something without understanding the return on investment.
So the challenge with the tax — as appealing as it is — is that it might make the “purchase decision” of funding Open Source easier, but it would probably make the distribution problem much worse.
Deliberate, intentional funding is key.  At least for the moment.
The current model we propose with our upcoming Open Source Pledge is to suggest like a tip what you should give in relation to your developer work force.
Using the size of the development team as a metric offers an objective and quantifiable starting point.
key_insight: Normalize open source funding with a measurable baseline tied to company scale, but keep allocation intentional because generic tax collection would misjudge value and readiness.
